scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE DISTRICT COURT / CIVIL COURT
AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT RELATING TO IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty-Five Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court.

LIMITATION

4. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from the date fixed for performance, or if no date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. That on 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into an Agreement to Sell regarding the residential flat bearing No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, bearing Survey No. {{PROPERTY_SURVEY_NUMBER}}, measuring 1200 sq ft, for a total consideration of Rs. 85,00,000/-. A copy of the Agreement to Sell is annexed hereto as **Annexure A**.

6. That pursuant to the said Agreement, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against execution of the sale deed. A copy of the RTGS receipt is annexed hereto as **Annexure B**.

7. That the Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contract and had arranged the balance funds to complete the transaction within the stipulated timeline.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

8. That despite the Plaintiff's readiness, the Defendant vide letter dated 05.06.2024 communicated his refusal to execute the sale deed, stating that he had received a higher offer for the subject property. A copy of the Defendant's refusal letter is annexed hereto as **Annexure D**.

9. That aggrieved by the Defendant's refusal, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the sale deed in terms of the Agreement. A copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt is annexed hereto as **Annexure C**.

10. That the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the said notice and has not executed the sale deed, thereby committing breach of the contract.

DAMAGES

11. That by reason of the Defendant's breach, the Plaintiff has been deprived of the property and is entitled to compensation for the loss suffered due to the wrongful detention of the advance amount and the non-execution of the sale deed.

LEGAL BASIS

12. The present cause is governed by Specific Relief Act, 1963, including Section 10 (mandatory after 2018 Amendment — court SHALL grant unless S.11(2), S.14, or S.16 exception), Section 16, Section 21, Section 22.

13. The present cause is governed by Indian Contract Act, 1872, including Section 10.

14. The Plaintiff also relies on Transfer of Property Act, 1882, including Section 53A, where the pleaded facts attract those provisions.

CAUSE OF ACTION

15. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

16. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 20.06.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

17. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

18. The suit is valued at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty-Five Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

19. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute and register the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
    (b) In the alternative, if specific performance is not granted, pass a decree for refund of the advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- with interest and compensation for damages;
    (c) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (d) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

Annexure D — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

VERIFICATION

Verified at Bengaluru on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, Anita Sharma, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 19 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

Anita Sharma

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership,
Having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

2. The suit is valued above the specified value as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

3. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

4. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

5. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

6. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when contract is broken; or for continuing breach, when it ceases. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. The Plaintiff states that pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 was conducted / is not required as urgent interim relief is being sought. The Plaintiff has complied with all mandatory procedural requirements under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system, with a total contract value of Rs. 45,00,000/-. The terms and conditions governing the rights and obligations of the parties are recorded in the said Agreement, marked as Annexure A.

9. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant as the first milestone payment on 01.02.2024 via NEFT. The transaction is evidenced by the receipt bearing UTR Number ICIC20240201456789, marked as Annexure B.

10. Under the terms of the Agreement, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024. The Plaintiff fulfilled all its preliminary obligations to facilitate the development work by the Defendant.

11. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024, thereby committing a breach of the contractual timeline stipulated in the Agreement.

12. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant communicated its decision to abandon the project entirely without completing the development work. This communication was conveyed via email correspondence, marked as Annexure C.

13. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance amount and compensate for the losses suffered. The Defendant has failed to reply to the said notice or return the advance payment, as evidenced by the legal notice and proof of delivery, marked as Annexure E.

DAMAGES

14. Due to the breach and abandonment of the project, the Plaintiff has suffered a total loss of Rs. 32,00,000/-. This comprises Rs. 20,00,000/- being the refundable advance payment and Rs. 12,00,000/- being consequential damages arising from the delay in operations caused by non-delivery. The assessment of consequential loss is supported by the loss assessment report prepared by the auditor, marked as Annexure D.

15. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest at the rate of 18% per annum on the outstanding amount from the date of breach until realization, as per the contractual terms and prevailing legal provisions.

LEGAL BASIS

16. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

17. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it.

CAUSE OF ACTION

18. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 01.05.2024 when the Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline, constituting breach of contract.

19. The cause of action further arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project entirely, and again on 01.07.2024 when the Defendant failed to comply with the legal notice dated 01.07.2024.

20. The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant has failed to refund the advance amount or compensate for losses despite repeated demands.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

21. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Karnataka Court Fees and Suits Valuation Act, 1958 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

22. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at 18% per annum or such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) towards damages, comprising:
        (i) Rs. 20,00,000/- towards refund of advance payment;
        (ii) Rs. 12,00,000/- towards consequential damages;
    (b) Award pendente lite and future interest at 18% per annum or such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (c) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (d) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024
Annexure B — NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789)
Annexure C — Email correspondence showing abandonment dated 15.06.2024
Annexure D — Loss assessment report by auditor
Annexure E — Legal notice dated 01.07.2024 and proof of delivery

VERIFICATION

Verified at Bengaluru on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, Priya Menon, the Director of TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd and authorised rep
